findings.claim: ADR-0004 and ADR-0006 are both Accepted (ADR-0004 is explicitly not marked superseded) yet they impose incompatible requirements on the 'loose' ADR dialect's required sections: ADR-0004 requires that loose-dialect ADRs be checked against zero required sections, while ADR-0006 requires REQUIRED_SECTIONS.loose to be `["context", "decision"]` — the same two-section requirement as the nygard dialect. A single codebase cannot simultaneously enforce 'no sections required' and 'context and decision sections required' for the same dialect classification.

citations.quote: Loose asserts zero required sections — a genuinely freer template shouldn't be measured against Nygard's structure at all.
`REQUIRED_SECTIONS.loose` reverts to `["context", "decision"]` — the same requirement as nygard.

findings.consequence: If both records are treated as currently governing (as their Accepted status implies), the D1 check's behavior for loose-dialect ADRs is ambiguous: an implementer or auditor consulting only ADR-0004 would configure zero required sections, while one consulting only ADR-0006 would configure two. A maintainer should either mark ADR-0004 as Superseded (at least with respect to its loose-dialect clause) or add an explicit amendment note to ADR-0004 pointing to ADR-0006, so the ADR log's status metadata matches what the text of ADR-0006 already asserts in prose.
